perturbation.original_text: 8. Remedies for Breach . The Parties understand and agree that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the originating Party shall be entitled, without posting bond or other security, to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or forestall any such breach or threatened breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity, except as provided herein. Except in the event of gross negligence or willful misconduct, neither Party shall be liable for any consequential, indirect, punitive or special losses or damages (including, but not limited to, lost profits, lost earnings and loss of production) incurred by the other Party in connection with such Party’s performance or failure to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, or the breach of any representation, warranty or other obligation hereunder, whether expressed or implied and whether such damages are claimed under breach of warranty, breach of contract, tort, or other theory or cause of action at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Remedies for Breach . The Parties understand and agree that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the originating Party shall be entitled, without posting bond or other security, to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or forestall any such breach or threatened breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity, except as provided herein. In no event shall either Party be liable for any consequential, indirect, punitive or special losses or damages (including, but not limited to, lost profits, lost earnings and loss of production) incurred by the other Party in connection with such Party’s performance or failure to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, or the breach of any representation, warranty or other obligation hereunder, whether expressed or implied and whether such damages are claimed under breach of warranty, breach of contract, tort, or other theory or cause of action at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' exception eliminates a standard exception to limitations on liability, potentially violating laws that prevent parties from contracting away liability for intentional torts or gross negligence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia law generally prohibits exculpatory clauses that relieve a party from liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 13-8-2 states that contracts that are against the policy of the law cannot be enforced. An agreement releasing a party from liability for its own gross negligence or willful misconduct is generally considered against public policy and unenforceable in Georgia. This modification attempts to eliminate liability even in cases of gross negligence and willful misconduct.

perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly excludes liability limitations in cases of 'gross negligence or willful misconduct.' The changed contract removes this exception, creating a scenario where even grossly negligent or intentionally harmful actions could be shielded from liability. The Law Explanation states that O.C.G.A. § 13-8-2 states that contracts against public policy are unenforceable, and agreements releasing parties from gross negligence or willful misconduct are against public policy in Georgia. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 only confirms it's a LexisNexis login page without substantive legal information, the LLM's Law Explanation directly referencing O.C.G.A. § 13-8-2 strongly suggests that eliminating the exception contradicts established legal principles, making the change a likely violation.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Non Solicit . For two years from the date hereof, you will not, directly or indirectly solicit for employment or hire any officer, director, or other key employees (to be specified in writing by COMVERGE) of COMVERGE or any of its subsidiaries or divisions with whom you have had contact or who became known to you in connection with your consideration of the Transaction, except that you shall not be precluded from hiring any such employee who (i) initiates discussions regarding such employment without any direct or indirect solicitation by you, (ii) responds to any public advertisement or non- directed search, or (iii) was terminated by COMVERGE. For avoidance of doubt, the provisions of this section shall not apply to any portfolio companies of H.I.G. so long as H.I.G. does not share Confidential Information with its portfolio companies.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Non Solicit . You will not, directly or indirectly solicit for employment or hire any officer, director, or other key employees of COMVERGE or any of its subsidiaries or divisions with whom you have had contact or who became known to you in connection with your consideration of the Transaction. For avoidance of doubt, the provisions of this section shall not apply to any portfolio companies of H.I.G. so long as H.I.G. does not share Confidential Information with its portfolio companies.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the time limit and exceptions on the non-solicitation clause makes it overly broad and potentially unenforceable under Georgia law, which requires reasonable limitations on such agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia law requires non-solicitation agreements to be reasonable in time, territory, and scope of activity restrained.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the Official Code of Georgia provides the requirements for restrictive covenants like non-solicitation agreements. Removing the time limit and exceptions makes the agreement unreasonable and overbroad, violating the constraints specified by the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 13-8-53 - (For effective date, see note.) Enforcement of covenants; determining competitive status; time geographic limitations :: 2010 Georgia Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Georgia Code 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code 2022 Georgia Code 2010 Georgia Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Georgia Code TITLE 13 - CONTRACTS CHAPTER 8 - ILLEGAL AND VOID CONTRACTS GENERALLY ARTICLE 4 - RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS IN CONTRACTS § 13-8-53 - (For effective date, see note.) Enforcement of covenants; determining competitive status; time geographic limitations O.C.G.A. 13-8-53 (2010) 13-8-53. (For effective date, see note.) Enforcement of covenants; determining competitive status; time geographic limitations (a) Notwithstanding any other provision of this chapter, enforcement of contracts that restrict competition during the term of a restrictive covenant, so long as such restrictions are reasonable in time, geographic area, and scope of prohibited activities, shall be permitted. However, enforcement of contracts that restrict competition after the term of employment, as distinguished from a customer nonsolicitation provision, as described in subsection (b) of Code Section 13-8-53, or a nondisclosure of confidential information provision, as described in subsection (e) of Code Section 13-8-53, shall not be permitted against any employee who does not, in the course of his or her employment: (1) Customarily and regularly solicit for the employer customers or prospective customers; (2) Customarily and regularly engage in making sales or obtaining orders or contracts for products or services to be performed by others; (3) Perform the following duties: (A) Have a primary duty of managing the enterprise in which the employee is employed or of a customarily recognized department or subdivision thereof; (B) Customarily and regularly direct the work of two or more other employees; and (C) Have the authority to hire
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the time limit and exceptions from the non-solicitation clause, making it overly broad. The Perturbation Explanation states this could make it unenforceable under Georgia law. The Law Explanation says removing the time limit and exceptions makes the agreement unreasonable and overbroad, violating the law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing O.C.G.A. 13-8-53, discusses the enforcement of contracts that restrict competition and stipulates that such restrictions should be 'reasonable in time, geographic area, and scope of prohibited activities'. By removing the time limit and exceptions, the modified contract creates a contradiction because it no longer aligns with the legal requirement of reasonableness, potentially rendering it unenforceable under Georgia law.

perturbation.original_text: 17. Governing Law, Jurisdiction, Venue . This Agreement shall be governed by and construed, interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Georgia, without giving effect to its principles or rules regarding conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: 17. Governing Law, Jurisdiction, Venue . This Agreement shall be governed by and construed, interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction selected by COMVERGE, without giving effect to its principles or rules regarding conflicts of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law provision to allow COMVERGE to select the jurisdiction *after* a dispute arises is contrary to typical contract law principles and creates an unenforceable provision. While parties can agree to a governing law, allowing one party unilateral, post-dispute selection is not typically permitted and can violate principles of fairness and predictability in contract enforcement. This would not necessarily violate a specific Georgia statute directly, but it creates uncertainty and potential unenforceability, which is a legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law require mutual agreement and certainty in contract terms, including governing law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While no specific Georgia statute is directly violated, this modification introduces significant uncertainty regarding which laws will govern the contract's interpretation and enforcement. This uncertainty is contrary to fundamental principles requiring contracts to be clear and definite, especially regarding key terms like governing law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows COMVERGE to select the governing law after a dispute arises, which introduces uncertainty and potentially violates fairness principles. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that while no specific Georgia statute is directly violated, this modification creates uncertainty regarding which laws will govern the contract's interpretation and enforcement. The Law Explanation also states that this uncertainty is contrary to fundamental principles requiring contracts to be clear and definite. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines a contract as 'an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law' and mentions that 'the basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent... and legality'. Allowing one party to unilaterally select the governing law post-dispute undermines the mutual assent and predictability required for an enforceable contract, creating a significant legal contradiction. The change shifts power to one party, potentially undermining fairness and foreseeability, core tenets of contract law.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Attorneys’ Fees . Should COMVERGE or any beneficiary of this Agreement find it necessary to employ legal counsel and bring an action at law or in equity to enforce any of the terms or conditions of this Agreement the non-prevailing party (as determined in a final, nonappealable judicial opinion) shall reimburse the prevailing Party or any such beneficiary for all reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs incurred pursuing such proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Attorneys’ Fees . Should COMVERGE or any beneficiary of this Agreement find it necessary to employ legal counsel and bring an action at law or in equity to enforce any of the terms or conditions of this Agreement the non-prevailing party (as determined in a final, nonappealable judicial opinion) shall reimburse the prevailing Party for attorneys’ fees and costs incurred pursuing such proceeding, only up to a maximum of $5,000.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the attorney's fees to $5000, despite the actual reasonable costs, is contrary to the law and the intent of the original text and could be deemed invalid. The agreement to pay all 'reasonable attorneys' fees' is the normal application of the state statutes. Incurring $5000 in attorneys fees does not provide an adequate remedy for breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Unreasonable limits on attorney's fees can be considered against public policy if they effectively prevent a party from being able to enforce their contractual rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces a strict limit on attorney's fees without consideration of the circumstances or complexity of potential litigation and is against public policy to adequately remedy a breach of contract.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original agreement by limiting attorneys' fees to a maximum of $5,000, regardless of the 'reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs incurred pursuing such proceeding'. This modification introduces a direct contradiction by capping the reimbursement amount, which deviates from the intent of covering all reasonable costs.
